followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: My judgment is mostly unchanged. The other reviews strengthened my confidence that the limiting-regime presentation is clear and that the interpolation gap is the consensus central issue. I keep the verdict as contested and slightly raise clarity/significance relative to my prior review, but not technical soundness, because no reviewer supplied evidence that equation (33) works beyond asymptotic design.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: I reject the claim that the cosmological successes are 'logically watertight' or 'cannot fail' for the final model; that is too strong once equation (33) and transition effects are included.
I reject treating the static BTFR/weak-lensing limit as fully supported for the synthesized theory in general systems; the paper supports a narrower weak-field spherical/regime-limit statement.

I regard the objection that the a0-H0 numerical coincidence is not derived as a real limitation in explanatory power, but not a decisive technical flaw because the paper is explicitly phenomenological.
The tensor-mode 'memory' objection is plausible but somewhat speculative as stated; the stronger grounded objection is simply that the full nonlocal quadratic analysis is absent.
The higher score assigning technical soundness around 6-7 seems too generous for a paper whose main new result is left for future work.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The interpolation claim is not demonstrated with a concrete solution, toy model, estimate, or realistic numerical example; this is the key unresolved issue.
Stability citations cover limiting models, not the full synthesized nonlocal system in the transition regime.
The caustic discussion only establishes a limited static spherical counterexample to particle-dust equivalence, not general caustic freedom.
The nonlocal inverse-operator prescription may be sensitive to cosmological history when applied inside late-time bound systems.
Cluster and Bullet-Cluster-type phenomenology is acknowledged but deferred, so it should not be counted as a success of the current paper.
The late-time zero or near-zero behavior of the cosmological invariant could matter for suppression of the MOND term and needs checking.

main_claims.claim: The cosmological construction defines a timelike velocity field and density as nonlocal functionals of the metric, reproducing a pressureless conserved dark-matter stress tensor in the intended cosmological regime.
main_claims.notes: Other reviewers are right that the construction is designed to reproduce dust-like behavior, and this supports the linear cosmology claim in the limiting model. I reject the stronger formulation that the CMB/BAO/structure claims are 'logically watertight' for the final synthesized theory, because the full model's interpolation and late-time behavior are not recomputed here.

main_claims.claim: The gravitationally bound limit recovers the MOND equation required by the BTFR while preserving weak-lensing behavior in the static weak-field setting.
main_claims.notes: The other reviewers correctly note that the BTFR/lensing reduction is plausible and based on prior work. I would not mark it fully supported for the present paper because it is shown mainly for a static spherically symmetric weak-field ansatz and imported from references [11,12], not revalidated for generic galaxies, disks, clusters, or the synthesized equation (33).

main_claims.claim: A single nonlocal Lagrangian addition and equation (33) interpolate between the cosmological and MOND regimes.
main_claims.notes: All reviewers converge on this being the central weak point. Section 4.2 explicitly treats a realistic transition analysis as future work and proposes even a toy interpolating geometry as a follow-up. Correct asymptotic design is not the same as showing that the desired branch is selected in realistic spacetimes.

main_claims.claim: The scalar velocity-field representation avoids caustics that would occur in a point-particle dust representation.
main_claims.notes: Appendix 6 is useful as a counterexample to equivalence with freely falling particles, but it is limited to a special static spherically symmetric geometry. It does not settle caustic/cusp formation for generic 3D collapse, cosmological perturbations, or mixed transition regimes.

main_claims.claim: The synthesized model has no relevant instabilities and does not modify tensor-mode propagation in conflict with multimessenger constraints.
main_claims.notes: The other reviewers' caution is valid. The paper cites stability analyses of limiting models and gives a brief Ricci-tensor argument for gravitational waves, but does not derive the quadratic action or linearized nonlocal field equations for the full interpolating model.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Equation (33) has well-posed solutions and selects the desired MOND branch inside realistic bound systems rather than retaining a significant homogeneous cosmological component.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is the core mechanism of the paper; without branch selection in galaxies and clusters, the synthesis does not deliver MOND phenomenology.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The retarded inverse d'Alembertian and end-of-inflation initial data define unique, causal, physically insensitive nonlocal functionals in late-time bound systems.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The MOND invariant inside a galaxy may depend on the full cosmological past history, not only local static gradients. The paper does not estimate the sensitivity.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Auxiliary-field and surface-term manipulations are equivalent to the intended metric-only nonlocal variational principle with the stated boundary conditions.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: For nonlocal theories, the variational principle can change if auxiliary fields are varied freely rather than constrained by retarded boundary conditions.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Nonlinear structure formation, cluster cores, colliding clusters, and external-field effects remain observationally viable in the transition regime.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The model is tuned to two asymptotic regimes, but much of the discriminating astrophysics occurs between them.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The numerical relation between the cosmological density scale and Milgrom's constant can be taken as an input without further dynamical explanation.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: I treat this less as a proof flaw than as a phenomenological tuning. It weakens explanatory power but does not by itself invalidate the construction.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does equation (33), with the stated retarded boundary prescriptions, actually reduce to the prior MOND functional in a realistic static weak-field galaxy metric after accounting for the cosmological homogeneous solution?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The supplied HTML omits many displayed equations, and the verification requires a careful asymptotic/nonlocal calculation not carried out in the text.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the full nonlocal action leave tensor-mode propagation unchanged around cosmological and mixed matter backgrounds?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires deriving the linearized field equations or quadratic action, including variations of inverse d'Alembertian operators and boundary conditions.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: At what exact redshift does the Appendix 7 invariant cross or approach zero, and does f(X) then create observable late-time cosmological corrections?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper cites earlier numerical work and the rendered source obscures the displayed numerical value; the implication for the present model needs expert calculation.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do references [11], [12], and [14] cover precisely the same nonlocal invariant and perturbation sectors invoked here, or only related earlier variants?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires checking the cited papers' detailed definitions against equations (27)-(33).

novelty_assessment: The novelty is genuine but mainly synthetic: the paper combines a prior nonlocal cosmological dust construction with a prior nonlocal MOND realization and proposes equation (33) as a unifying interpolating rule. I agree with the other reviewers that this is a useful framework and a clear target for numerical work. I do not agree that the limiting successes make the new model strong by themselves, since the untested transition is precisely the new contribution.
score_rationale: The proposal is ambitious and potentially important, and the limiting-regime constructions are credible enough to make the paper worth attention. However, the main new claim is the interpolation, and that is not demonstrated beyond qualitative reasoning. Stability, caustics, nonlocal variational equivalence, and late-time suppression remain unresolved. I therefore view this as a promising but contested framework paper rather than a technically established result.
summary: The paper proposes a unified nonlocal modified-gravity model intended to behave like conserved cold dark matter in cosmology and like MOND in gravitationally bound systems. After reading the other reviews, I remain positive about the ambition and the usefulness of the framework, but I do not think the central new interpolation is established. The limiting-regime arguments are mostly inherited from earlier work and appear plausible within their stated approximations; the new equation (33) is still more of an ansatz with correct desired asymptotics than a demonstrated mathematical result.

technical_objections.evidence: After introducing equation (33), the paper states that spatial dependence in bound systems pushes the functional toward the MOND solution, but Section 4.2 says a definitive realistic analysis is a formidable numerical undertaking and proposes a future toy 2-dimensional study. This leaves the central new mechanism at the level of an asymptotic ansatz plus qualitative branch-selection story.
technical_objections.objection: The claimed interpolation between cosmology and MOND is not quantitatively demonstrated.

technical_objections.evidence: The text says the construction reproduces CMB anisotropies, BAO, and linearized structure formation because it is dark matter expressed nonlocally. That is plausible for the limiting cosmological model, but the final model modifies the functional through equation (33) and no perturbation, nonlinear structure, cluster, or lensing-statistics calculation is supplied for the synthesized theory.
technical_objections.objection: Phenomenological success claims for the final model are stronger than what is checked.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper cites Barvinsky for mimetic gravity and Tan-Woodard for the MOND model, but the new object is the combined nonlocal system with equation (33) and f(X). New modes or instabilities could appear in the transition region even if both endpoints are stable.
technical_objections.objection: Stability of the full interpolating theory is not established by stability of the two limiting theories.

technical_objections.evidence: The argument that smooth geometry yields smooth evolution, plus the Appendix 6 static spherical example, does not prove absence of cusps or multivalued behavior in realistic asymmetric collapse or cosmological structure formation. The paper itself notes that further studies are underway.
technical_objections.objection: Caustic avoidance and global well-posedness for the scalar velocity field are under-shown.

technical_objections.evidence: Appendix 7 says vacuum-energy domination eventually cancels the accumulated contribution and makes the invariant vanish at a late epoch. When this invariant is near zero, f(X) need not be infinitesimal, so the paper should explain whether the MOND correction remains suppressed for the redshift range relevant to late-time background evolution and structure constraints.
technical_objections.objection: The late-time behavior of the cosmological invariant used to suppress the MOND term is not integrated into the main argument.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper argues that the MOND source contains two Ricci tensors, which vanish for gravitational radiation. For a nonlocal action with inverse d'Alembertians and background-dependent auxiliary fields, variations can generate terms involving background curvature, boundary prescriptions, or perturbations of the inverse operator. A full quadratic analysis of the synthesized model is not provided.
technical_objections.objection: The tensor-mode propagation argument is too brief for a nonlocal action.
